attributes.stem:
A 32-year-old woman presents to her General Practice clinic for a routine antenatal visit at 34 weeks gestation. This is her second pregnancy. She has no significant medical history and takes only pregnancy supplements. Her pregnancy has been uncomplicated to date.

On examination, she appears well. Her pulse rate is 82/min, blood pressure 118/75 mmHg, respiratory rate 16/min, SpO2 98% on room air, and temperature 36.7°C. Her cardiorespiratory and abdominal examinations are normal. There is no peripheral oedema.

Investigations reveal:

  • Haemoglobin: 122 g/L (110-150)
  • White cell count: 9.2 × 10⁹/L (4.0-11.0)
  • Platelet count: 95 × 10⁹/L (150-400)
  • Blood film: Reduced platelets. No schistocytes seen
  • Lactate dehydrogenase: 180 U/L (120-250)
  • Bilirubin: 12 µmol/L (3-20)
  • Alanine transferase: 22 U/L (5-40)
  • Creatinine: 65 µmol/L (45-90)
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with isolated mild thrombocytopenia, which was detected incidentally during routine antenatal screening. The absence of other abnormalities, particularly normal blood pressure, liver function, and no evidence of haemolysis (normal LDH, bilirubin and notably no schistocytes on blood film), makes gestational thrombocytopenia the most likely diagnosis. This is a common condition affecting up to 10% of pregnancies, typically occurring in the third trimester.

Thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura (TTP) is a thrombotic microangiopathy (TMA) characterised by thrombocytopenia and microangiopathic haemolytic anaemia (with schistocytes on blood film) due to ADAMTS13 deficiency. This patient has none of these features, and her lactate dehydrogenase and bilirubin levels are normal, making haemolysis unlikely.

Haemolytic uraemic syndrome (HUS) is a TMA which typically presents with the triad of thrombocytopenia, microangiopathic haemolytic anaemia, and acute kidney injury. This patient's normal renal function and absence of haemolysis make this diagnosis unlikely.

HELLP syndrome is characterised by Haemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, and Low Platelets. This patient's liver function tests are normal, and there is no evidence of haemolysis.

Pre-eclampsia is defined by new-onset hypertension after 20 weeks gestation with either proteinuria or evidence of end-organ dysfunction. This patient's blood pressure is normal, and there are no other features suggestive of pre-eclampsia.

Gestational thrombocytopenia typically presents with platelet counts above 70 × 10⁹/L, no associated medical conditions, and normal coagulation parameters. It is a diagnosis of exclusion that resolves spontaneously after delivery. Close monitoring of platelet counts is recommended, but specific treatment is usually not required.
learningPoints: Gestational thrombocytopenia is a diagnosis of exclusion characterised by mild thrombocytopenia occurring in late pregnancy without other complications.
The absence of haemolysis, liver dysfunction, and hypertension helps differentiate gestational thrombocytopenia from more serious conditions like HELLP syndrome.
Platelet count typically remains above 70 × 10⁹/L in gestational thrombocytopenia and resolves spontaneously after delivery.
references: Cines DB, Levine LD. Thrombocytopenia in pregnancy. Blood. 2017;130(21):2271-2277. doi:10.1182/blood-2017-05-781971
